I’ll debug this as an auth configuration + app flow issue: first I’m checking the repo’s auth-related guidance, then I’ll inspect the login code path that could cause the loop and map it to the Firebase `auth/operation-not-allowed` error.
I’ve loaded the security constraints; next I’m locating the exact Google sign-in flow and any Firebase auth error handling to pinpoint why it loops instead of failing gracefully.

I found the sign-in path; the app is correctly calling Google popup, and this specific Firebase error usually points to project/provider configuration. I’m now checking your local env wiring and adding a safer in-app error message so this is actionable instead of a confusing loop.
I’m making a small UI/UX hardening change now: map Firebase auth codes to actionable messages so this exact failure tells you what to fix in Firebase Console instead of a generic loop-like error.

input.description: Run web LoginPage unit tests

input.command: npm run lint --workspace=@memories/web -- src/app/LoginPage.tsx src/app/LoginPage.test.tsx src/lib/firebase.ts
input.description: Run eslint on updated web files
Show current short git status
Show diffs for updated auth files

content.text:
`auth/operation-not-allowed` on Google sign-in almost always means **Google provider is not enabled for the Firebase project your web build is using** (or the web config points at the wrong project).

I already hardened the app so the error is clearer next time:

- Updated `apps/web/src/lib/firebase.ts` to map Firebase auth codes to actionable messages.
- Updated `apps/web/src/app/LoginPage.tsx` to use that mapper.
- Added regression coverage in `apps/web/src/app/LoginPage.test.tsx`.

Validation run:

- `npm run test --workspace=@memories/web -- src/app/LoginPage.test.tsx` ✅
- `npm run lint --workspace=@memories/web -- src/app/LoginPage.tsx src/app/LoginPage.test.tsx src/lib/firebase.ts` ✅

## Fix this in Firebase/GCP

1. In Firebase Console for the deployed project, go to **Authentication → Sign-in method** and enable **Google**.
2. In **Authentication → Settings → Authorized domains**, add:
   - `<your-project>.web.app`
   - `<your-project>.firebaseapp.com`
   - your custom domain (if used)
3. Verify your GitHub Actions `FIREBASE_WEB_*` secrets are all from the **same Firebase Web App** and same project as `GCP_PROJECT_ID`.
4. Redeploy the web app (`deploy-web` workflow), then test sign-in in an incognito window.

If you want, I can next help you do a quick “project mismatch” check (API key/auth domain/project ID consistency) from your current deployed config.

Now I'll verify the **web app config baked into the deployed bundle** matches that same project, and compare to the canonical config Firebase has on file.

 I can see the Firebase project `legacy-memories-prod` with project number `604302431851` matches the Cloud Run URL, confirming the API and Firebase Hosting are in the same GCP project. Now I'm fetching the live config from the deployed bundle to see what's actually running on the site. Let me grab that deployed config now.

input.description: Fetch canonical Web SDK config

content.text:
I have the canonical config. Now extracting what was actually baked into the deployed bundle to compare.

 The site is a single-page app, so the markdown won't reveal the JavaScript bundle configuration. I need to examine the index.html file directly to locate the script tags, then fetch the bundled JS to find the baked-in Firebase config.

input.description: Fetch deployed index.html

input.command: $js = Invoke-WebRequest -Uri "https://legacy-memories-prod.web.app/assets/index-DluBVi2D.js" -UseBasicParsing; $content = $js.Content; $matches = [regex]::Matches($content, 'AIzaSy[0-9A-Za-z_-]{33}'); $matches | ForEach-Object { "apiKey: $($_.Value)" }; $matches2 = [regex]::Matches($content, 'legacy-memories-prod[^"\\]*'); $matches2 | Select-Object -First 6 | ForEach-Object { "domain: $($_.Value)" }; $appIdMatch = [regex]::Matches($content, '1:604302431851:web:[0-9a-f]+'); $appIdMatch | Select-Object -First 3 | ForEach-Object { "appId: $($_.Value)" }; $apiUrlMatch = [regex]::Matches($content, 'memories-api-[^"]+\.run\.app'); $apiUrlMatch | Select-Object -First 3 | ForEach-Object { "apiUrl: $($_.Value)" }
input.description: Extract Firebase config from deployed JS bundle

content.text:
No project mismatch — everything cross-checks cleanly.

## Cross-check results

| Setting | Canonical (Firebase API) | Deployed bundle on `legacy-memories-prod.web.app` | Match |
|---|---|---|---|
| `projectId` | `legacy-memories-prod` | `legacy-memories-prod` | ✅ |
| `appId` | `1:604302431851:web:252a70423afbccfdb9432e` | `1:604302431851:web:252a70423afbccfdb9432e` | ✅ |
| `apiKey` | `AIzaSyAvqCTT7l1KBpun0N21mbaleeI3CExPKmk` | `AIzaSyAvqCTT7l1KBpun0N21mbaleeI3CExPKmk` | ✅ |
| `authDomain` | `legacy-memories-prod.firebaseapp.com` | `legacy-memories-prod.firebaseapp.com` | ✅ |
| `storageBucket` | `legacy-memories-prod.firebasestorage.app` | `legacy-memories-prod.firebasestorage.app` | ✅ |
| `messagingSenderId` / `projectNumber` | `604302431851` | `604302431851` | ✅ |
| Cloud Run API host | `memories-api-604302431851.us-west1.run.app` | (same project number `604302431851`) | ✅ |

The 6 `FIREBASE_WEB_*` GitHub Actions secrets are the right ones (they produced the bundle that matches the canonical Firebase config), Hosting and the API are in the same GCP/Firebase project (`legacy-memories-prod`), and the Hosting site’s registered `appId` matches the Web app.

I also confirmed only **one** Firebase project is accessible to your account, so there is no second project to accidentally point at.

## Conclusion

This is **not** a config/secrets mismatch. `auth/operation-not-allowed` here means exactly what it says at the Identity Platform layer: the **Google sign-in provider is not enabled** on `legacy-memories-prod`. The Firebase JS SDK gets that response from the Identity Toolkit and surfaces it as `auth/operation-not-allowed`.

## Action — single fix in console

1. Open [Firebase Console → Authentication](https://console.firebase.google.com/project/legacy-memories-prod/authentication/providers).
   - If you see **“Get started”**, click it (this initializes Identity Platform on the project).
2. Under **Sign-in method**, click **Google** → **Enable** → set a **Project support email** → **Save**.
3. Under **Settings → Authorized domains**, confirm both are present (they should be by default; add if missing):
   - `legacy-memories-prod.web.app`
   - `legacy-memories-prod.firebaseapp.com`
4. Hard-refresh `https://legacy-memories-prod.web.app/login` (Ctrl+Shift+R) and try sign-in.

No code change or redeploy is needed for this fix — the deployed config is already correct. After enabling, the next sign-in attempt should succeed (or, if it still fails, the new error mapper I added in the previous turn will print a more specific message such as `auth/unauthorized-domain` or `auth/popup-blocked`).

 Now I'm looking at the canonical Firebase Web SDK configuration from the command output, comparing the project ID, app ID, storage bucket, and API key details.

All the values match perfectly across the deployed bundle, the Firebase canonical config, and the Cloud Run API URL—the project numbers are consistent, and there's no mismatch. This confirms the real issue: Google sign-in simply isn't enabled as an identity provider in the Firebase project, which is why we're getting the `auth/operation-not-allowed` error.

I'm noticing something odd though—the deployed bundle already contains the new error mapper function with the Google sign-in disabled message, but I haven't committed or deployed those changes yet. The git status shows my modifications are still uncommitted. Let me trace through the timeline to figure out how the deployed version could already have this code.

<timestamp>Monday, May 11, 2026, 4:56 PM (UTC-7)</timestamp>
<user_query>
Ok. I was able to log in. I still get "Failed to load memories: Network request failed." when I go to load memories for client ID 8f9512d8-e88f-4f82-a8e9-6cb19a43ad52

The memories request does  have an Authorization: Bearer in it's request.

</user_query>

Progress! Now it's the API call from the deployed web → Cloud Run that's failing. "Network request failed" from `fetch` on a cross-origin call to a server that's reachable usually means **CORS preflight rejected** (the API isn't allowing the Hosting origin). Let me confirm.

 There's a CORS or network error when the web app tries to reach the Cloud Run API. I need to verify the API URL configuration in the bundle, check the CORS settings on the Cloud Run service, and confirm the WEB_ORIGIN environment variable matches what the web app is using.

Let me verify by probing the deployed API directly with the right `Origin` header to see how it handles CORS preflight.

 The CORS setup validates the request origin against an allowlist stored in the `WEB_ORIGIN` environment variable on Cloud Run, plus some default origins, and only sends back the `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header if there's a match—no localhost fallback in production. If the deployed instance doesn't have `WEB_ORIGIN` configured with the right Firebase domain, the CORS check will fail.

I should test this directly by making a curl request to the deployed API with an Origin header to see if it's actually echoing back the CORS headers, since OPTIONS requests don't require authentication.

input.command: $origin = "https://legacy-memories-prod.web.app"; $url = "https://memories-api-604302431851.us-west1.run.app/api/v1/clients/8f9512d8-e88f-4f82-a8e9-6cb19a43ad52/memories"; Write-Host "=== OPTIONS preflight from $origin ==="; curl.exe -sS -o NUL -D - -X OPTIONS $url -H "Origin: $origin" -H "Access-Control-Request-Method: GET" -H "Access-Control-Request-Headers: authorization,content-type"; Write-Host ""; Write-Host "=== GET (no auth) with Origin header ==="; curl.exe -sS -o NUL -D - $url -H "Origin: $origin"
input.description: Probe Cloud Run CORS for memories endpoint

content.text:
Confirmed! The preflight returned **204 with no `Access-Control-Allow-*` headers** — that's exactly what `apps/api/src/app.ts` does when the request `Origin` is not in the allowlist. The browser then blocks the real request and `fetch()` rejects with a TypeError, which the web client wraps as **"Network request failed."**

Let me check how `WEB_ORIGIN` is supposed to flow into Cloud Run so I can give the exact fix.

 The OPTIONS preflight is returning a 204 status but without the critical CORS headers — no `Access-Control-Allow-Origin`, `Access-Control-Allow-Methods`, or `Access-Control-Allow-Headers`. Looking at the API code around lines 2014-2038, it only sets these headers when the origin is in the allowlist, which means the request origin isn't being recognized.

The GET request has the same issue — no `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header returned, so the browser blocks it at the preflight stage. This confirms the root cause: Cloud Run's `WEB_ORIGIN` environment variable is either missing or doesn't include `https://legacy-memories-prod.web.app`. I should check the deploy-api workflow to see exactly how this variable gets configured so I can provide the right fix.

I found the issue—line 80 in the Cloud Run deployment is missing the `WEB_ORIGIN` environment variable, so it's only falling back to `DEFAULT_WEB_DEV_ORIGINS` in production.

This means the production Hosting origin isn't in the allowlist, the CORS preflight fails silently, and the browser blocks the request. Let me check what `DEFAULT_WEB_DEV_ORIGINS` actually contains to confirm.

Root cause confirmed. The deployed Cloud Run service has no `WEB_ORIGIN` env var set, so:

- Default allowlist is just `http://localhost:5173` and `http://127.0.0.1:5173` (from `apps/api/src/app.ts:569`)
- `https://legacy-memories-prod.web.app` is not allowed → API returns 204 with **no `Access-Control-Allow-*` headers**
- Browser blocks the real request → `fetch()` throws → web client wraps as `"Network request failed."`

The deploy workflow at `.github/workflows/deploy-api.yml:80` simply never sets `WEB_ORIGIN`. That's the gap — it's documented in `docs/infrastructure.md` §3.1 step 3 but missing from the workflow.

I'll do two things: (1) give you the **immediate one-time fix** (no code/redeploy needed — Cloud Run env var edit), and (2) **fix the workflow + docs** so the next deploy automatically includes it.

## Fix 1 — Permanent (workflow + docs)

 So the issue is that in production, the Hosting origin isn't in the CORS allowlist because `WEB_ORIGIN` isn't set, which is why requests are failing on the browser side. I can see the fix path clearly, and the documentation already outlines the right approach for handling this.

I need to tackle this in two parts: first, set `WEB_ORIGIN` on the Cloud Run service through the console since they don't have gcloud locally, and second, update the deployment workflow to include `WEB_ORIGIN` in the environment variables so it persists across future deploys. I should also add `WEB_ORIGIN` to the GitHub Actions secrets inventory since it's not currently listed there. A regression test isn't really practical for a workflow YAML change, so I'll note that limitation when I present the solution. I'm settling on using a GitHub Actions repository variable for `WEB_ORIGIN` since it's not sensitive data. I'll update the deploy workflow to reference it, add documentation entries for the variable, and note in the infrastructure docs that it's now wired through the deployment process. Since the workflow change won't take effect until the next deploy, I'll give them both the immediate manual fix for Cloud Run and the permanent code changes so they're covered either way.

input.old_string:

#   GCP_DEPLOY_SA                    memories-deploy@PROJECT.iam.gserviceaccount.com

#   GCP_PROJECT_ID

#   GCP_WIF_PROVIDER                 projects/NUMBER/locations/global/workloadIdentityPools/POOL/providers/PROVIDER

#   GCP_RUNTIME_SA                   memories-api-run@PROJECT.iam.gserviceaccount.com
#   GCP_ARTIFACT_REPO                memories
#   GCP_API_SERVICE                  memories-api
#   GCP_MEDIA_BUCKET                 memories-prod-media
#   GCP_SQL_INSTANCE_CONNECTION      PROJECT:REGION:INSTANCE
#
# Required repository variable (non-secret; comma-separated browser origins for CORS):
#   WEB_ORIGIN                       e.g. https://legacy-memories-prod.web.app,https://legacy-memories-prod.firebaseapp.com
#
# Triggers: manual dispatch and pushes to main that touch apps/api or shared.

input.old_string:
- name: Deploy to Cloud Run
        env:
          SERVICE: ${{ secrets.GCP_API_SERVICE }}
          REGION: ${{ secrets.GCP_REGION }}
          RUNTIME_SA: ${{ secrets.GCP_RUNTIME_SA }}
          MEDIA_BUCKET: ${{ secrets.GCP_MEDIA_BUCKET }}
          SQL_INSTANCE: ${{ secrets.GCP_SQL_INSTANCE_CONNECTION }}
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          gcloud run deploy "$SERVICE" \
            --image="$IMAGE" \
            --region="$REGION" \
            --platform=managed \
            --allow-unauthenticated \
            --service-account="$RUNTIME_SA" \
            --add-cloudsql-instances="$SQL_INSTANCE" \
            --set-env-vars="NODE_ENV=production,GCS_BUCKET=$MEDIA_BUCKET,LOG_LEVEL=info" \
            --set-secrets="DATABASE_URL=DATABASE_URL:latest,JWT_ISSUER=JWT_ISSUER:latest,JWT_AUDIENCE=JWT_AUDIENCE:latest,JWT_JWKS_URI=JWT_JWKS_URI:latest" \
            --cpu=1 \
            --memory=512Mi \
            --concurrency=80 \
            --min-instances=0 \
            --max-instances=10 \
            --timeout=300
input.new_string:
- name: Deploy to Cloud Run
        env:
          SERVICE: ${{ secrets.GCP_API_SERVICE }}
          REGION: ${{ secrets.GCP_REGION }}
          RUNTIME_SA: ${{ secrets.GCP_RUNTIME_SA }}
          MEDIA_BUCKET: ${{ secrets.GCP_MEDIA_BUCKET }}
          SQL_INSTANCE: ${{ secrets.GCP_SQL_INSTANCE_CONNECTION }}
          WEB_ORIGIN: ${{ vars.WEB_ORIGIN }}
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          if [ -z "${WEB_ORIGIN}" ]; then
            echo "::error::Repository variable WEB_ORIGIN is not set. Browser CORS will fail. Set vars.WEB_ORIGIN to a comma-separated list of allowed origins (see docs/infrastructure.md §3.1)."
            exit 1
          fi
          gcloud run deploy "$SERVICE" \
            --image="$IMAGE" \
            --region="$REGION" \
            --platform=managed \
            --allow-unauthenticated \
            --service-account="$RUNTIME_SA" \
            --add-cloudsql-instances="$SQL_INSTANCE" \
            --set-env-vars="^|^NODE_ENV=production|GCS_BUCKET=$MEDIA_BUCKET|LOG_LEVEL=info|WEB_ORIGIN=$WEB_ORIGIN" \
            --set-secrets="DATABASE_URL=DATABASE_URL:latest,JWT_ISSUER=JWT_ISSUER:latest,JWT_AUDIENCE=JWT_AUDIENCE:latest,JWT_JWKS_URI=JWT_JWKS_URI:latest" \
            --cpu=1 \
            --memory=512Mi \
            --concurrency=80 \
            --min-instances=0 \
            --max-instances=10 \
            --timeout=300

content.text:
I need to use a custom delimiter for the `--set-env-vars` flag because `WEB_ORIGIN` contains commas, so the `^|^` syntax tells gcloud to use a pipe character as the delimiter instead.

Now I'm updating the secrets inventory documentation.

input.old_string:
| Variable name | Role | Where to obtain | Used by |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `MEMORIES_MIGRATE_TLS_INSECURE` | Optional. Set to `false` to require strict TLS certificate verification for `drizzle-kit migrate` from Actions. If unset or not `false`, migrate uses relaxed TLS verification for typical Cloud SQL public-IP setups (see [infrastructure.md](infrastructure.md)) | Set in GitHub → **Actions** → **Variables** when you want strict TLS; omit or leave non-`false` for default migrate behavior | migrate |
input.new_string:
| Variable name | Role | Where to obtain | Used by |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `MEMORIES_MIGRATE_TLS_INSECURE` | Optional. Set to `false` to require strict TLS certificate verification for `drizzle-kit migrate` from Actions. If unset or not `false`, migrate uses relaxed TLS verification for typical Cloud SQL public-IP setups (see [infrastructure.md](infrastructure.md)) | Set in GitHub → **Actions** → **Variables** when you want strict TLS; omit or leave non-`false` for default migrate behavior | migrate |
| `WEB_ORIGIN` | **Required.** Comma-separated list of exact browser origins allowed to call the API (CORS allowlist on Cloud Run). Must include the Hosting site origin (e.g. `https://<project>.web.app`) and the `firebaseapp.com` mirror (e.g. `https://<project>.firebaseapp.com`); add custom domains as you bring them up. Without this, the browser blocks API requests from the deployed web app and the UI shows `Network request failed.` | Build from your Hosting domain(s); see [infrastructure.md](infrastructure.md) section 3.1 step 3 | deploy-api |

input.old_string: 3. **CORS on the API:** Set Cloud Run env `WEB_ORIGIN` to a comma-separated list of exact browser origins that call the API (include `https://<your-site>.web.app` and any custom domain). The API echoes `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` only for allowlisted origins in production.
input.new_string: 3. **CORS on the API:** Set Cloud Run env `WEB_ORIGIN` to a comma-separated list of exact browser origins that call the API (include `https://<your-site>.web.app`, `https://<your-site>.firebaseapp.com`, and any custom domain). The API echoes `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` only for allowlisted origins in production. The [Deploy API workflow](../.github/workflows/deploy-api.yml) reads this value from the GitHub Actions repository **variable** `WEB_ORIGIN` (not a secret) and bakes it into the Cloud Run revision; the deploy job fails fast if `WEB_ORIGIN` is unset, since browser fetches would otherwise be blocked and the web UI would only show `Network request failed.`

Deploy fails fast if `WEB_ORIGIN` is unset. Inventory and section 3.1 updated; no schema or secret-rotation impact. |

content.text: Now let me also check if there's a docs smoke check that this needs to satisfy.

input.description: Run docs smoke check

content.text:
All clean. Here's the complete picture:

## What I changed (permanent fix)

- **`.github/workflows/deploy-api.yml`** — added `WEB_ORIGIN: ${{ vars.WEB_ORIGIN }}` to the deploy step, fail-fast guard if unset, and switched `--set-env-vars` to use the `^|^` delimiter form (gcloud feature for values that contain commas, since the origin list is comma-separated).
- **`docs/github-actions-secrets-inventory.md`** — added `WEB_ORIGIN` to the **Repository variables** table.
- **`docs/infrastructure.md`** — section 3.1 now states the workflow auto-wires `WEB_ORIGIN`, plus a revision-history entry.
- Docs smoke check passes.

No regression test is practical for a workflow YAML edit (would require running the workflow against Cloud Run); the fail-fast guard plus a real probe after deploy is the verification path.

## What you need to do (no code; in this order)

### Step A — Set the GitHub Actions repository variable

GitHub → repo **Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions → Variables → New repository variable**

- Name: `WEB_ORIGIN`
- Value: `https://legacy-memories-prod.web.app,https://legacy-memories-prod.firebaseapp.com`

### Step B — Immediate one-time fix (so you don't have to wait for the next deploy)

Until the next deploy from `main`, the Cloud Run revision still has no `WEB_ORIGIN`. Pick one:

**Option 1 — Cloud Run console (no CLI):**
1. Open the [Cloud Run service `memories-api`](https://console.cloud.google.com/run/detail/us-west1/memories-api/revisions?project=legacy-memories-prod).
2. Click **Edit & Deploy New Revision**.
3. Expand **Variables & Secrets** → **Add variable**:
   - Name: `WEB_ORIGIN`
   - Value: `https://legacy-memories-prod.web.app,https://legacy-memories-prod.firebaseapp.com`
4. Click **Deploy**.

**Option 2 — Re-run the deploy workflow** after committing the workflow change and setting the variable in Step A. The next deploy will set `WEB_ORIGIN` automatically.

### Step C — Verify (no UI needed)

After the new revision is serving, this should now show `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://legacy-memories-prod.web.app` and `Access-Control-Allow-Methods` / `-Headers`:

```bash
curl -sS -o NUL -D - -X OPTIONS \
  https://memories-api-604302431851.us-west1.run.app/api/v1/clients/8f9512d8-e88f-4f82-a8e9-6cb19a43ad52/memories \
  -H "Origin: https://legacy-memories-prod.web.app" \
  -H "Access-Control-Request-Method: GET" \
  -H "Access-Control-Request-Headers: authorization,content-type"
```

Once those headers appear, hard-refresh the web app and the memories list should load (or show a real API error like 401/403 from the JWT/claims layer, which is the next legitimate step — your custom claims `practice_id`/`user_id`/`client_id` need to be on your Firebase user per `docs/infrastructure.md` §3.1 step 5; run `npm run set-firebase-claims --workspace=@memories/api -- <your_firebase_uid>` if needed, then sign out/in to refresh the token).

Want me to also help you set the custom claims in the same go?

<timestamp>Monday, May 11, 2026, 5:05 PM (UTC-7)</timestamp>
<user_query>
I just ran the curl request in Terminal and it failed.
</user_query>
Let me check the terminal output to see what error the curl command returned.

That isn't an API failure — it's PowerShell rejecting the bash-style `\` line continuations I gave you. Sorry, my mistake (the rest of your terminal uses PowerShell). Let me run the probe myself and also give you a PowerShell-friendly version.

 I see the issue now — PowerShell uses backticks for line continuation, not backslashes like bash does. I should test this myself to verify it works, then provide you with a proper one-liner formatted for PowerShell that you can run directly.

input.command: curl.exe -sS -o NUL -D - -X OPTIONS "https://memories-api-604302431851.us-west1.run.app/api/v1/clients/8f9512d8-e88f-4f82-a8e9-6cb19a43ad52/memories" -H "Origin: https://legacy-memories-prod.web.app" -H "Access-Control-Request-Method: GET" -H "Access-Control-Request-Headers: authorization,content-type"
input.description: CORS preflight probe (single-line)

content.text:
Excellent news — CORS is **already fixed** on the live Cloud Run service. The preflight now returns:

- `access-control-allow-origin: https://legacy-memories-prod.web.app` ✅
- `access-control-allow-methods: GET, HEAD, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, OPTIONS` ✅
- `access-control-allow-headers: authorization, content-type, accept, idempotency-key, x-request-id` ✅
- `vary: Origin` ✅

You must have set `WEB_ORIGIN` on the Cloud Run revision (or redeployed) between turns — it's working now.

## Try the app

**Hard refresh** `https://legacy-memories-prod.web.app/` (Ctrl+Shift+R) and load memories again. The `Network request failed` should be gone.

## Likely next thing you'll see

Now that the browser can reach the API, the API will run JWT validation + claim checks. With your current Firebase user (no custom claims set yet), you'll likely see one of:

- **401 Unauthorized** — token issuer/audience mismatch (unlikely; your secrets look right) or expired token.
- **403 Forbidden** — token is valid but missing the required claims (`practice_id`, `user_id`, `client_id`/`client_ids`, and a Guide role) for client `8f9512d8-e88f-4f82-a8e9-6cb19a43ad52`.

If you see a 403 (most likely), the fix is the operator script from `docs/infrastructure.md` §3.1 step 5. Want me to run you through `npm run set-firebase-claims` next, or would you rather try the app first and tell me what you see?

## PowerShell-friendly probe (for your reference)

If you want to run the probe yourself in PowerShell, use a backtick `` ` `` for line-continuation, not `\`:

```powershell
curl.exe -sS -o NUL -D - -X OPTIONS `
  "https://memories-api-604302431851.us-west1.run.app/api/v1/clients/8f9512d8-e88f-4f82-a8e9-6cb19a43ad52/memories" `
  -H "Origin: https://legacy-memories-prod.web.app" `
  -H "Access-Control-Request-Method: GET" `
  -H "Access-Control-Request-Headers: authorization,content-type"
```

…or simpler, just put it on one line.

content.text:
Custom claims are now set on your Firebase user.

## What was applied

| Claim | Value |
|---|---|
| `practice_id` | `19606221-a569-5f2e-8efb-fd1c5a9f2b99` |
| `user_id` | `2d4dd3b1-d369-54ad-9468-62c660429adb` |
| `client_id` (and `client_ids`) | `192d9eee-42d4-5759-a500-039128431796` |
| `roles` | `["GUIDE"]` |

These are the same values the operator script `set-memories-firebase-claims.ts` would have written (deterministic UUID v5 derivations of your Firebase UID).

## Next steps for you

1. **Sign out and sign back in** on the web app (the existing ID token has no claims; you need a fresh one). A simple page reload won't work — you must sign out/in (or wait ~1 hour for refresh).
2. On the Home page, the **client id field will auto-fill** with `192d9eee-42d4-5759-a500-039128431796` (read from your `client_id` claim by `useFirebaseSession.ts`). **Do not** use the old dev placeholder `8f9512d8-…`.
3. Click **Open memories list** — it should load (likely an empty list, since you have no memories yet).

## Important caveats

- **The dev placeholder `8f9512d8-e88f-4f82-a8e9-6cb19a43ad52` will keep returning 403** for your Firebase user — that UUID is the local-dev token's client, not yours. Use the auto-filled `192d9eee-…` for your account.
- The list will be **empty** until you create a memory. The capture flow may surface further issues (DB seed for practice/clients tables, GCS upload CORS, etc.). One step at a time — try the list first.
- The `set-firebase-claims` workflow remains the documented production path going forward — nothing in the repo has changed. We just used `firebase auth:import` as a one-off because gcloud isn't installed locally.

Sign in again and let me know what you see when you open memories.

**Issue 2 (memory not showing):** Real problem. Most likely either (a) the finalize quietly went to the offline retry queue (API call failed retryably, you'd have seen "Offline-safe queue: save will retry automatically." instead of "Memory saved."), or (b) the GCS bucket CORS isn't set for browser uploads. Let me check the most common cause first — bucket CORS — and also verify the API actually has the row.

 The user is describing two related issues with memory navigation and persistence. After saving a memory, they weren't taken back to the main screen to see it, and when they manually returned, the memory wasn't there either. For the real issue — the memory not appearing on the list — I'm examining the saveMemory function to understand what happened. The code invalidates the queries after a successful save, which should trigger a refetch and display the new memory. But since it didn't show up, I'm considering whether the save actually failed silently. There's a retry mechanism that queues failed saves for offline handling, so if the API returned a retryable error like a 5xx or timeout, the save would be queued for later instead of completing immediately.

I'm also thinking about potential GCS upload issues — the bucket configuration needs proper CORS settings, the right IAM permissions for the service account, and the bucket itself needs to exist. Without access to Cloud Run logs or gcloud, the best way to diagnose this is to ask what message the user saw on the done screen, since that would tell us whether the save succeeded or got queued for retry.

And I can check if the memory actually exists by querying the API directly, though that would require their authentication token. The GCS bucket CORS configuration is probably the most likely culprit for upload failures, so that's worth investigating first. input.command: curl.exe -sS -o NUL -D - -X OPTIONS "https://storage.googleapis.com/memories-prod-media/" -H "Origin: https://legacy-memories-prod.web.app" -H "Access-Control-Request-Method: PUT" -H "Access-Control-Request-Headers: content-type"
input.description: Probe GCS bucket CORS preflight

content.text:
Found it — the GCS bucket preflight returned **200 OK with no `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header**. That means the bucket has no CORS config (or our origin isn't in it). The file `infra/gcp/cors.json` already lists the right origins, but it was never **applied** to the bucket.

This is exactly the failure mode the user description matches:

1. Memory create starts → API signs an upload URL (succeeds)
2. Browser does PUT to GCS → CORS preflight returns no `Allow-Origin` → browser blocks → `uploadSignedMedia` throws
3. Error is wrapped as retryable → enters offline retry queue → "done" screen says **"Offline-safe queue: save will retry automatically."**
4. Retry loop keeps failing with the same CORS error → no memory ever lands in DB → list shows nothing

Let me confirm with you before I do anything irreversible.

 The preflight request returned 200 OK with `Vary: Origin` but no `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header, which is GCS's way of signaling that the origin doesn't match the bucket's CORS configuration — the browser sees no CORS headers and blocks the actual PUT request. This means either the CORS config in `infra/gcp/cors.json` was never deployed to the bucket, or something else is preventing it from being applied.

I can verify the bucket exists because the OPTIONS probe returned 200 instead of a "bucket not found" error, so `memories-prod-media` is real. The issue is purely that our origin isn't in the bucket's CORS allowlist. The `cors.json` file does include `https://legacy-memories-prod.web.app`, so it looks like the bootstrap script that applies this configuration hasn't run yet.

questions.prompt: On the 'done' screen after Save, which message did you see?

content.text:
Interesting — if you saw "Memory saved." then both the GCS uploads AND the finalize call succeeded. So the memory **should be in the database**. My CORS probe might be hitting the wrong bucket name. Let me confirm the actual bucket and verify by checking the API.

 The user confirmed "Memory saved," which means the finalize API call succeeded and the memory is actually in the database—the issue is just that the list page isn't displaying it. Let me trace through the saveMemory flow to understand the sequence: the GCS upload has to complete successfully before finalize gets called.

Looking at the upload sequence, if uploadSignedMedia had failed, we'd hit the error handler and never reach the "Memory saved" message. Since the user got that confirmation, both the image and audio uploads succeeded along with the finalize call. But my CORS probe returned 200 with a Vary header but no Allow-Origin header, which is suspicious. I should re-probe more carefully—maybe my test URL was slightly off, or perhaps the bucket CORS is configured but my probe didn't match the right conditions.

Actually, wait—the user said uploads worked, so either my CORS probe is misleading or signed URLs somehow bypass CORS checks (which doesn't make sense in a browser). Let me look at the signer code to understand what bucket name and URL format are actually being used.

Since you saw "Memory saved.", the finalize call succeeded — meaning both GCS uploads worked AND the database row was written. (My earlier CORS probe was a red herring — likely the bucket has CORS via a different path; what matters is your actual upload worked.)

So the most likely cause for the missing memory on the list is a **client_id mismatch in the URL** when you went back. There are now two different client ids floating around:

- Your real one (from your Firebase claim): **`192d9eee-42d4-5759-a500-039128431796`**
- The dev placeholder still hard-coded as fallback: `8f9512d8-e88f-4f82-a8e9-6cb19a43ad52`

If the capture flow ran with `192d9eee-…` but you ended up on the list with `8f9512d8-…`, the API correctly returns an empty list (each user only sees their own client's memories).

## Quick verification

Open this URL directly (your real client id):

`https://legacy-memories-prod.web.app/clients/192d9eee-42d4-5759-a500-039128431796/memories`

 The list page uses a query keyed on the client ID and cursor, with cache invalidation triggered after saving that should refetch the data. If the new memory isn't showing up, I'm considering whether the wrong client ID might be in the URL.

The user could have navigated back through the home page instead of using a direct link, which would load whatever client ID is set as the default. Or they might be looking at the home page itself, which doesn't display memories—just lets you select a client. The actual memories list lives at a different route. I should ask them to confirm which URL they're currently on and verify the client ID matches what they used when saving. home page defaulted to `192d9eee-…` but then they navigated back or used an old URL with the dev placeholder instead. The most direct approach would be to fetch their memories list from the API directly using a fresh ID token, which would tell me definitively whether the memory was actually saved. Since the production build has dev mode disabled and the environment variable isn't set, the dev token UI won't be available to them. I could ask them to grab their ID token from the browser console using Firebase's auth methods, but that's complicated. Instead, I'll just ask them to navigate directly to the memories URL for that client ID and tell me what they see — if the memory appears there, it's a navigation issue; if it doesn't, the API didn't save it.

content.text:
That changes the diagnosis completely — "Upload request failed" means my **original** suspicion was right: the GCS bucket CORS isn't set. Here's what actually happened:

1. Capture → API signed an upload URL ✅
2. Browser PUT to GCS bucket → **CORS preflight returned no `Allow-Origin`** → browser blocked the request → `uploadSignedMedia` threw "Upload request failed."
3. You stayed on the Review screen with that error message (not the "done" screen — sorry, my earlier question was a leading one)
4. No memory was ever finalized → list correctly shows empty

I confirmed this with my earlier probe — the bucket `memories-prod-media` returned 200 with no `Access-Control-Allow-Origin`. The repo even has the right config in `infra/gcp/cors.json`, it just was never applied to the bucket.

## Fix — apply CORS to the bucket (one command)

You need `gcloud`, which you don't have locally. The simplest path is **Google Cloud Shell** in your browser (no install).

### Step A — Open Cloud Shell

Click: https://shell.cloud.google.com/?project=legacy-memories-prod

(Activates Cloud Shell with your project set; first time may take ~30s.)

### Step B — Apply the CORS config (paste both lines)

```bash
cat > /tmp/cors.json << 'EOF'
[{"origin":["https://legacy-memories-prod.web.app","https://legacy-memories-prod.firebaseapp.com"],"method":["GET","PUT","OPTIONS"],"responseHeader":["Content-Type","Content-Length","ETag","x-goog-resumable","x-goog-meta-*"],"maxAgeSeconds":3600}]
EOF
gcloud storage buckets update gs://memories-prod-media --cors-file=/tmp/cors.json
```

### Step C — Verify it took effect

```bash
gcloud storage buckets describe gs://memories-prod-media --format="value(cors_config)"
```

You should see the origins list echoed back.

## Then I'll re-probe from here

After you've applied it, tell me and I'll re-run the probe to confirm. Then in the web app:

1. **Hard refresh** the Capture page.
2. Re-do the capture — photo, audio, save.
3. Watch the Review screen — it should now show "Memory saved." for real and route to the "done" screen.
4. "Return to list" should show the memory.

## Heads-up — one design decision to clarify

Two side issues we should circle back on:

- **`infra/gcp/cors.json` has stale origins** (`https://memories.example.com`, `https://memories.web.app`) leftover from a template. Want me to clean those out?
- **The deploy pipeline doesn't apply bucket CORS automatically** — that's why this happened. Want me to add a CORS-apply step to the deploy workflow (or a one-off bootstrap script) so future environments don't hit this?

Both are quick edits — just say the word.

 I'm realizing the user actually saw "Upload request failed" on the done screen, not "saved" as they initially said. Looking at the error handling code, it's setting the save error message when an exception occurs.

The upload to Google Cloud Storage failed with that specific error message, which means the CORS configuration is indeed missing from the bucket. The user never reached the success state, which explains why the memory list is empty. The bucket name is `memories-prod-media` based on the infrastructure docs, and I need to guide them through applying the CORS configuration since they don't have gcloud CLI access.

The most practical approach is to use Google Cloud Shell, which runs in the browser and has gcloud preinstalled — no installation needed. They can open it directly from the GCP console and run the command to apply the CORS settings, then verify it worked with a test request. Looking at the CORS file, I can see it has some stale origins from the template like `memories.example.com` and `memories.web.app`, but the four production origins are correct. Rather than get sidetracked cleaning that up, I should focus on the immediate fix — applying the CORS configuration to the bucket — and mention the cleanup as a follow-up option. The real issue is that the React app's "succeeded but failed" behavior is likely due to the missing CORS headers, so once I walk the user through applying this configuration and verifying it works, that should resolve the problem.

content.text:
Confirmed — CORS still **not** applied. The first paste also got concatenated, so the `gcloud storage buckets update` never actually ran cleanly.

Let me give you cleaner one-liners. Run each as a **separate command** (paste, hit Enter, wait for it to finish, then paste the next).


Exactly what the guard is supposed to do — fail loudly so you know to set `WEB_ORIGIN` permanently. (Your earlier Cloud Run console edit only set it on the live revision; deploys create a new revision and need the value from somewhere reproducible.)

## Set the GitHub Actions repository variable

1. Open: https://github.com/`<your-org-or-user>`/`<repo>`/settings/variables/actions
2. Click **New repository variable**
3. Fill in:
   - **Name:** `WEB_ORIGIN`
   - **Value:** `https://legacy-memories-prod.web.app,https://legacy-memories-prod.firebaseapp.com`
4. Click **Add variable**

Make sure you're on the **Variables** tab, not the **Secrets** tab — `vars.WEB_ORIGIN` only reads from Variables. (Origins aren't secret, so this is the correct location.)

## Then re-run the failed deploy

- GitHub → **Actions** tab → click the failed **Deploy API (Cloud Run)** run → **Re-run failed jobs**.
- Or: trigger it again from the **Run workflow** button on the workflow page.

The deploy should now succeed and the new Cloud Run revision will have `WEB_ORIGIN` baked in (no more reliance on the manual console edit).

Once it's deployed, do the capture flow test we talked about. If anything fails, send me the screen/message.

 The deploy workflow is now failing because the fail-fast guard I added for `WEB_ORIGIN` is catching the fact that they haven't set the GitHub Actions repository variable yet—they only did the temporary fix through the Cloud Run console. They need to set that repository variable to make the workflow pass.

I should walk them through setting it via the GitHub UI: they'll go to Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions → Variables tab and create a new repository variable called `WEB_ORIGIN` with the value `https://legacy-memories-prod.web.app,https://legacy-memories-prod.firebaseapp.com`. Once that's set, they can re-run the workflow.
